draft.introduction: The Suffering Servant passage, the single most important Old Testament text for early Christian theology. The servant bears the sins of others, is crushed for their iniquities, and by his wounds they are healed.

translation.text: Who has believed what he has heard from us? And to whom has the arm of the Lord been revealed? He was despised and rejected by men, a man of sorrows and acquainted with grief. Surely he has borne our griefs and carried our sorrows. He was pierced for our transgressions; he was crushed for our iniquities; upon him was the chastisement that brought us peace, and with his wounds we are healed.
translation.original_text: κύριε τίς ἐπίστευσεν τῇ ἀκοῇ ἡμῶν

translation.text: He was oppressed, and he was afflicted, yet he opened not his mouth; like a lamb that is led to the slaughter. By oppression and judgment he was taken away. Yet it was the will of the Lord to crush him; he shall see his offspring; he shall prolong his days. Out of the anguish of his soul he shall see and be satisfied.
translation.original_text: καὶ αὐτὸς διὰ τὸ κεκακῶσθαι οὐκ ἀνοίγει τὸ στόμα

translator_notes.note: The Ethiopian eunuch in Acts 8:32-35 asks Philip to explain this passage, and Philip “beginning from this Scripture, told him the good news about Jesus.” This is the earliest recorded christological interpretation of Isaiah 53, and it became the standard Christian reading.
